segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd been three days out of Ashford when he smelled the smoke -- the wrong kind, not hearth-fire or forge-smoke but the flat, mineral bite of stone that had changed its mind about being stone.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd been heading nowhere in particular, the way a man does when the war is over and the shape of peacetime hasn't settled yet. A hedge-knight's work: a missing horse here, a disputed boundary there. Small things. Useful things.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Things that didn't ask him to be more than he was.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Then the smoke.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Thornwick still smoked when Caelin rode in at first light.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Ash muffled the cobbles, the horse's hooves swallowed by the soft texture and depth of the soot-packed street. Melted stone and hot glass mingling with the acrid bite of charred timber.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Houses hadn't burned so much as slumped: wooden beams turned translucent, doorframes sagging into glossy curves, window mullions weeping clear droplets that froze mid-fall.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Even the lane stones had softened and set again, their surfaces ridged like cooled lava.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He'd seen dragonfire before -- in the war, villages reduced to char and ash in minutes. But this was different. This fire hadn't consumed. It had transformed.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: A cry -- thin, desperate, trapped -- cut through the silence.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He ran.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: At the end of the south lane, a cottage had folded in on itself like a paper lantern crushed by an invisible fist.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Hold on!"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin called, dropping to his knees in the debris.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Where are you?"

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Here,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: a small voice gasped, barely audible through the rubble.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "It's hot -- please -- "

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He found her beneath a collapsed roof beam: maybe eight years old, freckled face streaked with soot, pinned by timber that still shimmered with residual heat. Her eyes were wide with terror, tracking the glow in the wood above her.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You're going to be all right,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he said, setting his palm against the scorching beam.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He called the flame the way he always had -- shaping heat to eat along the grain, to reduce wood to ash and set her free. But the magic answered differently this time. The beam didn't char or crumble. It softened.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Fibers gave way beneath his touch, going glassy and viscous, sloughing under his hand in a slow, honey-thick peel. He drew the beam aside in a translucent sheet; the instant it left contact with his skin, it hardened with a sharp crack and shattered.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The girl stared up at him with wonder and fear in equal measure.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He stared at his own hand.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Fire shouldn't do this,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: he murmured, more to himself than to her.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He gathered the child up in his arms, feeling how she trembled against him.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "What's your name?"

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Mira."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I'm Caelin. Close your eyes now. The air's bad out here."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Outside, where the square's wind provided some relief from the smoke, she curled into his travel cloak like it was the only safe thing left in the world. Her lips trembled as she tried to form words.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Mama said to hide,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: she whispered finally.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "But the red lady saw me anyway. She spoke to me."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "The dragon?"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin asked, though something in his chest already knew the answer.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Mira nodded, her small fingers clutching his cloak tighter.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "She said she was looking for the ember-born. She said she was sorry but she had to know."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The child's voice dropped to barely audible.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Then she sang something. A rhyme. Over and over while the fire came. I... I remember it."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Before he could respond, Elder Gareth emerged from the smoke -- one cheek blistered raw, his beard singed away on that side, but his eyes still steady with the kind of strength that comes from bearing witness to catastrophe.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "You saved one,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: the old man rasped, his voice thick with smoke and grief.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "The gods bless you for it."

segments.speaker: mira
segments.text: "She wasn't hunting,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Mira whispered against Caelin's shoulder.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "She kept saying she was sorry. That she had to find someone."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Gareth nodded grimly.

segments.speaker: mira
segments.text: "Dragons kill clean when they feed -- quick, then gone. This was deliberate. Methodical. She melted the shrine down to its foundation stones but left the granary untouched. Destroyed the inn but spared the healer's cottage."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: His eyes met Caelin's.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "She was testing. Calling out in the old tongue, waiting for something to answer."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Something prickled along Caelin's forearms, a readiness sharpening his awareness. He'd spent years studying fragments of Draconic in dusty libraries, and his dreams had carried sounds and syllables that weren't his own.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Now the anticipation felt physical, like standing on the edge of a precipice, waiting for the moment gravity takes hold.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Mira tugged at his sleeve with surprising insistence.

segments.speaker: mira
segments.text: "The rhyme. Do you want to hear it?"

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "Please,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin said, though the answer was already pressing against his ribs like a held breath.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The child swallowed hard and recited in a thin, earnest voice:

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nine the arts and nine the lights,

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Nine the souls to set them right;

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Keep the chain and guard the frame --

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Wake not the Tenth that has no name.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: The words settled into him like keys finding locks, fitting into spaces he hadn't known were waiting to be filled.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Gareth pointed beyond the mill with a hand that trembled slightly.

segments.speaker: mira
segments.text: "Her trail ends past the hedgerow. The ground there is..."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He struggled for words.

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "The earth has turned to glass. Black glass, like obsidian. There's a crater, and at the center..."

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He shook his head.

segments.speaker: mira
segments.text: "You should see it yourself."

segments.speaker: caelin
segments.text: "I'll go,"

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: Caelin said, because any other response felt like turning away from something that had been calling him for years.

segments.speaker: narrator
segments.text: He didn't look back at Mira. He was afraid if he did he would stay, and staying felt like the wrong kind of cowardice.
